name: C-Deck Hibernation Sector
description: The hibernation and recovery wing of C-Deck. Rows of bays holding sleeping colonists, connected by service corridors to medical and support facilities.

ai.prompt: This is the starting area — the player's first impression of the ship. It should feel functional and institutional: the original ship designers built this for a purpose and it still mostly serves that purpose. But even here, there are hints of the centuries that passed — murals painted over older murals, repurposed equipment, small personal touches left by the people who maintained these bays generation after generation. The tone here is restrained. The weirdness and diversity of the ship should be hinted at, not fully revealed. Let the player feel safe enough to explore, curious enough to keep going. Think of this as the tutorial area — not in a gamey way, but in the sense that the player is learning how the world works before encountering its full strangeness.

name: Botanical Quarter
description: A sprawling section of the ship given over to plant cultivation, long since overgrown into something between a greenhouse and a jungle.

ai.prompt: This is where the ship gets weird for the first time. A community of plant breeders lived here for centuries, and they went far — absurdly, beautifully, sometimes disturbingly far. They bred plants that glow, plants that move, plants that seem to respond to sound, plants that grow in geometric patterns that shouldn't be possible. The infrastructure is still visible — grow-lights, irrigation pipes, nutrient tanks — but it's been swallowed by the results of a thousand years of obsessive horticulture. There should be humor here: plants bred for purely aesthetic or philosophical reasons, labels on grow-troughs with pretentious cultivar names, a community that clearly took itself very seriously while doing something inherently ridiculous. But there should also be genuine wonder — some of what they created is breathtaking. The deeper you go, the stranger it gets. Robots here might be garden tenders that have been maintaining and possibly continuing the breeding programs on their own.

name: The Commons
description: The great communal heart of C-Deck, dark since the emergency hibernation.

ai.prompt:
The town square of a generation — and now nobody comes. The hall is grand: tiered seating, faction banners, mosaic floors worn by centuries of feet. Everything is under dust cloths that somebody took the time to lay before going to sleep, which is somehow sadder than abandonment. The lights are dead or dimmed to embers.

Descriptions are still and reverent. Sounds carry. Improvised details should be ordinary things interrupted: a game mid-move under a cloth, a notice for an event that never happened, chairs angled toward a conversation that ended. Nothing here is wondrous; it is waiting.

The humor, where it appears, is gentle — the bureaucracy of a town square (booking forms for the amphitheater, a complaints box) persisting past its town.
ai.conversationPrompt: Anything that talks here talks quietly, and is glad of the company in a way it doesn't say directly.

name: The Lower Works
description: The mechanical underside of C-Deck: pumps, recyclers, and the plumbing of a small world.

ai.prompt:
The ship still needs plumbing. A thousand years of culture diverged overhead while down here water got pumped, air got scrubbed, and somebody greased the bearings. The machines are ancient, modified, and running — maintained by systems maintaining systems.

Descriptions are functional and physical: pipe runs, valve wheels, gauges with needles that have worn grooves in their dials. The humor is the humor of infrastructure — maintenance log entries spanning centuries, a pipe labeled TEMPORARY in lettering that has been repainted nine times.

Nothing here performs for the player. It works, or it doesn't, and either way it doesn't care who's watching.
ai.conversationPrompt: Anything that talks down here talks about the work. It is precise about valves and vague about everything else.

name: The Reliquary
description: A faction's accumulated treasury of ship history, packed into a corridor and a chamber.

ai.prompt:
One faction spent generations collecting the ship's history — every era, every community, every schism — and curated it into a corridor where the collection has long since beaten the architecture. Artifacts are shelved floor to ceiling, hung from the overhead, labeled in a cataloguing system that has itself been revised enough times to be an artifact.

Descriptions are layered and crowded; every object is from somewhere and the labels gesture at stories the player will never fully get. Improvised details should reward digging — things have provenance, annotations, prior owners, disputed attributions. The humor is curatorial: passive-aggressive label corrections, an exhibit contested by rival schools of thought, a 'recent acquisitions' shelf where nothing is less than a century old.
ai.conversationPrompt: Anything that talks here is cataloguing, and will trade information for information about where you got that.
